URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation

SUMMARY:
The Arrhenius equation describes the exponential dependence of the rate constant of a chemical reaction on the absolute temperature as k \= A e − E a R T , {\displaystyle k=Ae^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}},}  where The rate constant is then calculated as ⁠ k \= z A B e − E a R T {\displaystyle k=z_{AB}e^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}}  ⁠, so that the collision theory predicts that the pre-exponential factor is equal to the collision number zAB. However for many reactions this agrees poorly with experiment, so the rate constant is written instead as ⁠ k \= ρ z A B e − E a R T {\displaystyle k=\rho z_{AB}e^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}}  ⁠.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Formula for temperature dependence of rates of chemical reactions

In [physical chemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physical_chemistry "Physical chemistry"), the **Arrhenius equation** is a formula for the temperature dependence of [reaction rates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reaction_rate "Reaction rate"). The equation was proposed by [Svante Arrhenius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Svante_Arrhenius "Svante Arrhenius") in 1889, based on the work of Dutch chemist [Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacobus_Henricus_van_%27t_Hoff "Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff") who had noted in 1884 that the [Van 't Hoff equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_%27t_Hoff_equation "Van 't Hoff equation") for the temperature dependence of [equilibrium constants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equilibrium_constant "Equilibrium constant") suggests such a formula for the rates of both forward and reverse reactions. This equation has a vast and important application in determining the rate of chemical reactions and for calculation of [energy of activation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy"). Arrhenius provided a physical justification and interpretation for the formula.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Arrhenius96-1)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Arrhenius226-2)[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Laidler1984-3)[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Laidler42-4) Currently, it is best seen as an [empirical relationship](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empirical_relationship "Empirical relationship").[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Connors-5): 188  It can be used to model the temperature variation of [diffusion coefficients](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mass_diffusivity "Mass diffusivity"), population of [crystal vacancies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vacancy_defect "Vacancy defect"), [creep](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Creep_(deformation) "Creep (deformation)") rates, and many other thermally induced processes and reactions.\[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_\] The [Eyring equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eyring_equation "Eyring equation"), developed in 1935, also expresses the relationship between rate and energy.

Formulation
-----------

\[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Arrhenius_equation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Formulation")\]

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/NO2_Arrhenius_k_against_T.svg/250px-NO2_Arrhenius_k_against_T.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:NO2_Arrhenius_k_against_T.svg)

In almost all practical cases, E a ≫ R T {\\displaystyle E\_{a}\\gg RT} ![Image 5: {\displaystyle E_{a}\gg RT}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/390ea01ec37b6c842bdc61ff8d981eedd77b088d) and _k_ increases rapidly with _T_.

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/KineticConstant.png/250px-KineticConstant.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:KineticConstant.png)

Mathematically, at very high temperatures so that E a ≪ R T {\\displaystyle E\_{a}\\ll RT} ![Image 7: {\displaystyle E_{a}\ll RT}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/26e87774abb33538d077c324cfe28305366d262b) , _k_ levels off and approaches _A_ as a limit, but this case does not occur under practical conditions.

The Arrhenius equation describes the [exponential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_function "Exponential function") dependence of the [rate constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rate_constant "Rate constant") of a chemical reaction on the [absolute temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_temperature "Absolute temperature") as k \= A e − E a R T , {\\displaystyle k=Ae^{\\frac {-E\_{\\text{a}}}{RT}},} ![Image 8: {\displaystyle k=Ae^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7f80a4013b9643c215d2247c1113f5d5801e7ffe) where

*   k is the [rate constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rate_constant "Rate constant") (frequency of collisions resulting in a reaction),
*   T is the [absolute temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_temperature "Absolute temperature"),
*   A is the [pre-exponential factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pre-exponential_factor "Pre-exponential factor") or Arrhenius factor or frequency factor. Arrhenius originally considered A to be a temperature-independent constant for each chemical reaction.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-6) However more recent treatments include some temperature dependence – see _[§ Modified Arrhenius equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#Modified_Arrhenius_equation)_ below.
*   _E_a is the molar [activation energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") for the reaction,
*   R is the [universal gas constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_gas_constant "Universal gas constant").[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Arrhenius96-1)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Arrhenius226-2)[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-Laidler42-4)

Alternatively, the equation may be expressed as k \= A e − E a k B T , {\\displaystyle k=Ae^{\\frac {-E\_{\\text{a}}}{k\_{\\text{B}}T}},} ![Image 9: {\displaystyle k=Ae^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{k_{\text{B}}T}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1a9ac5d759c4ff14cefce68009884e32929d41cd) where

*   _E_a is the [activation energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") for the reaction (in the same unit as _k_B_T_),
*   _k_B is the [Boltzmann constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boltzmann_constant "Boltzmann constant").

The only difference is the unit of _E_a: the former form uses energy per [mole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mole_(unit) "Mole (unit)"), which is common in chemistry, while the latter form uses energy per [molecule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") directly, which is common in physics. The different units are accounted for in using either the [gas constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gas_constant "Gas constant"), R, or the [Boltzmann constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boltzmann_constant "Boltzmann constant"), _k_B, as the multiplier of temperature T.

The unit of the pre-exponential factor A are identical to those of the rate constant and will vary depending on the order of the reaction. If the reaction is first order it has the unit [s](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second "Second")−1, and for that reason it is often called the _[frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") factor_ or _attempt frequency_ of the reaction. Most simply, k is the number of collisions that result in a reaction per second, A is the number of collisions (leading to a reaction or not) per second occurring with the proper orientation to react[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-7) and e − E a R T {\\displaystyle e^{\\frac {-E\_{\\text{a}}}{RT}}} ![Image 10: {\displaystyle e^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd9bd39c4c9d0260f62e9ef50a02f1f224d1082e) is the probability that any given collision will result in a reaction. It can be seen that either increasing the temperature or decreasing the activation energy (for example through the use of [catalysts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catalyst "Catalyst")) will result in an increase in rate of reaction.

Given the small temperature range of kinetic studies, it is reasonable to approximate the activation energy as being independent of the temperature. Similarly, under a wide range of practical conditions, the weak temperature dependence of the pre-exponential factor is negligible compared to the temperature dependence of the factor ⁠ e − E a R T {\\displaystyle e^{\\frac {-E\_{\\text{a}}}{RT}}} ![Image 11: {\displaystyle e^{\frac {-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd9bd39c4c9d0260f62e9ef50a02f1f224d1082e) ⁠; except in the case of "barrierless" [diffusion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diffusion "Diffusion")\-limited reactions, in which case the pre-exponential factor is dominant and is directly observable.

With this equation it can be roughly estimated that the rate of reaction increases by a factor of about 2 to 3 for every 10 °C rise in temperature, for common values of activation energy and temperature range.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-8)

The e − E a R T {\\displaystyle e^{\\frac {-E\_{a}}{RT}}} ![Image 12: {\displaystyle e^{\frac {-E_{a}}{RT}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/51ae81ab29a510dbfc95899885fb3e4d88bc932a) factor denotes the fraction of molecules with energy greater than or equal to E a {\\displaystyle E\_{a}} ![Image 13: {\displaystyle E_{a}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/46a5c4699f75d498b504e59bfd8b5496aa20351a) .[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrhenius_equation#cite_note-9)

Derivation
----------

\[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Arrhenius_equation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Derivation")\]

# 6.2.3.2: The Arrhenius Equation

![ ](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/123564/clark.jpg?revision=3 "Jim Clark")

This page examines rate constant variation with temperature and activation energy, as shown by the Arrhenius equation.

## Rate Constants and Rate Equations

The rate equation for a reaction between two substances, A and B, is the following:

![reqnab1.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16950/reqnab1.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=365&height=158)

![reqnab1.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16950/reqnab1.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=365&height=158)

The rate equation shows the effect of changing the reactant concentrations on the rate of the reaction. All other factors affecting the rate—temperature and catalyst presence, for example—are included in the rate constant, which is only constant if the only change is in the concentration of the reactants. If the temperature is changed or a catalyst is added, for example, the rate constant changes. This is shown mathematically in the Arrhenius equation:

![arrhenius1.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16951/arrhenius1.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=339&height=188)

![arrhenius1.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16951/arrhenius1.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=339&height=188)

The various symbols represent the following:

The Arrhenius equation often takes this alternate form, generated by taking the natural logarithm of the standard equation:

\[ \large \ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E\_a}{RT} \nonumber \]

## Temperature Effects on Chemical Kinetics

The Arrhenius equation can be used to determine the effect of a change of temperature on the rate constant, and consequently on the rate of the reaction. If the rate constant doubles, for example, so does the rate of the reaction.

##### Example \(\PageIndex{1}\):

What is the kinetic effect of increasing temperature from 20°C to 30°C (293 K to 303 K)?

###### Solution

The frequency factor, A, is approximately constant for such a small temperature change. This problem concerns the quantity e-(EA / RT), the fraction of molecules with energies equal to or in excess of the activation energy.

Let's assume an activation energy of 50 kJ mol-1, or, equivalently, J mol-1. The value of the gas constant, R, is 8.31 J K-1 mol-1.

At 20 °C (293 K) the value of the fraction is:

![fraction1.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16952/fraction1.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=193&height=80)

![fraction1.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16952/fraction1.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=193&height=80)

Raising the temperature (to 303 K) increases the quantity:

![fraction2.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16954/fraction2.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=193&height=80)

![fraction2.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16954/fraction2.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=193&height=80)

You can see that the fraction of the molecules able to react has almost doubled by increasing the temperature by 10°C. The rate of reaction is nearly doubled.

## The Effect of a Catalyst

A catalyst provides a reaction route with a lower activation energy. Suppose that the catalyzed activation energy is to 25 kJ mol-1. The calculation is repeated at 293 K:

![fraction3.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16956/fraction3.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=193&height=80)

![fraction3.gif](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/16956/fraction3.gif?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=193&height=80)

Compared with the corresponding value for an activation energy of 50 kJ mol-1, there is a significant increase in the fraction of molecules able to react. There are almost 30,000 times more molecules which can react in the presence of the catalyst compared to having no catalyst (using our assumptions about the activation energies). These calculations can also be done in reverse: given the rate of reaction or rate constants at several temperatures, the activation energy can be calculated.

It is common knowledge that chemical reactions occur more rapidly at higher temperatures. Milk turns sour much more rapidly if stored at room temperature rather than in a refrigerator; butter goes rancid more quickly in the summer than in the winter; and eggs hard-boil more quickly at sea level than in the mountains. For the same reason, cold-blooded animals such as reptiles and insects tend to be more lethargic on cold days.

The reason for this is not hard to understand. Thermal energy relates direction to motion at the molecular level. As the temperature rises, molecules move faster and collide more vigorously, greatly increasing the likelihood of bond cleavages and rearrangements. Whether it is through the [collision theory](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Physical_and_Theoretical_Chemistry_Textbook_Maps/Supplemental_Modules_(Physical_and_Theoretical_Chemistry)/Kinetics/06%3A_Modeling_Reaction_Kinetics/6.01%3A_Collision_Theory "Physical Chemistry/Kinetics/Rate Laws/Gas Phase Kinetics/Collision Theory"), transition state theory, or just common sense, chemical reactions are typically expected to proceed faster at higher temperatures and slower at lower temperatures.

![Image 8: arrhenius_lab.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/17844/arrhenius_lab.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=284&height=332)

By 1890 it was common knowledge that higher temperatures speed up reactions, often doubling the rate for a 10-degree rise, but the reasons for this were not clear. Finally, in 1899, the Swedish chemist Svante Arrhenius (1859-1927) combined the concepts of activation energy and the Boltzmann distribution law into one of the most important relationships in physical chemistry:

![Image 9: arrhenius_eqn_exp.png](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/17845/arrhenius_eqn_exp.png?revision=1)

Take a moment to focus on the meaning of this equation, neglecting the _A_ factor for the time being. First, note that this is another form of the exponential decay law discussed in the previous section of this series. What is "decaying" here is not the concentration of a reactant as a function of time, but the magnitude of the rate constant as a function of the exponent _–E_ a /_RT_. And what is the significance of this quantity? Recalling that _RT_ is the _average kinetic energy_, it becomes apparent that the exponent is just the ratio of the activation energy _E_ a to the average kinetic energy. The larger this ratio, the smaller the rate (hence the negative sign). This means that high temperature and low activation energy favor larger rate constants, and thus speed up the reaction. Because these terms occur in an exponent, their effects on the rate are quite substantial.

The two plots below show the effects of the activation energy (denoted here by _E_‡) on the rate constant. Even a modest activation energy of 50 kJ/mol reduces the rate by a factor of 10 8.

![Image 10: arrhenius_plots2.png](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/17847/arrhenius_plots2.png?revision=1)![Image 11: arrhenius-T.png](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/17848/arrhenius-T.png?revision=1)

Figure 6.2.3.1.1 6.2.3.1.1: Arrhenius plots. The logarithmic scale in the right-hand plot leads to nice straight lines.

Looking at the role of temperature, a similar effect is observed. (If the _x_-axis were in "kilodegrees" the slopes would be more comparable in magnitude with those of the kilojoule plot at the above right.)

Determining the Activation Energy
---------------------------------

The Arrhenius equation,

k=A e−E a/R T(6.2.3.1.1)(6.2.3.1.1)k=A e−E a/R T

can be written in a non-exponential form that is often more convenient to use and to interpret graphically. Taking the logarithms of both sides and separating the exponential and pre-exponential terms yields

ln k=ln(A e−E a/R T)=ln A+ln(e−E a/R T)=(−E a R)(1 T)+ln A(6.2.3.1.2)(6.2.3.1.3)(6.2.3.1.4)(6.2.3.1.2)ln⁡k=ln⁡(A e−E a/R T)(6.2.3.1.3)=ln⁡A+ln⁡(e−E a/R T)(6.2.3.1.4)=(−E a R)(1 T)+ln⁡A

Equation [6.2.3.1.4](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Physical_and_Theoretical_Chemistry_Textbook_Maps/Supplemental_Modules_(Physical_and_Theoretical_Chemistry)/Kinetics/06:_Modeling_Reaction_Kinetics/6.02:_Temperature_Dependence_of_Reaction_Rates/6.2.03:_The_Arrhenius_Law/6.2.3.01:_Arrhenius_Equation#mjx-eqn-3)6.2.3.1.4 is in the form of y=m x+b y=m x+b - the equation of a straight line.

ln k=ln A−E a R T ln⁡k=ln⁡A−E a R T

where temperature is the independent variable and the rate constant is the dependent variable. So if one were given a data set of various values of k k, the rate constant of a certain chemical reaction at varying temperature T T, one could graph ln(k)ln⁡(k) versus 1/T 1/T. From the graph, one can then determine the slope of the line and realize that this value is equal to −E a/R−E a/R. One can then solve for the activation energy by multiplying through by -R, where R is the gas constant.

This affords a simple way of determining the activation energy from values of _k_ observed at different temperatures, by plotting ln k ln⁡k as a function of 1/T 1/T.

##### Example 6.2.3.1.1 6.2.3.1.1: Isomerization of Cyclopropane

For the isomerization of cyclopropane to propene,

![Image 12: cyclopropisom_eqn.png](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/17851/cyclopropisom_eqn.png?revision=1)

the following data were obtained (calculated values shaded in pink):

![Image 13: cyclopropisom_plot.png](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/17850/cyclopropisom_plot.png?revision=1)

| T, °C | 477 | 523 | 577 | 623 |
| --- |
| 1/_T_, K–1 × 10 3 | 1.33 | 1.25 | 1.18 | 1.11 |
| _k_, s–1 | 0.00018 | 0.0027 | 0.030 | 0.26 |
| ln _k_ | –8.62 | –5.92 | –3.51 | –1.35 |

From the calculated slope, we have

–(E a R)E a=–3.27×10 4 K=–(8.314 J m o l–1 K–1)(–3.27×10 4 K)=273 k J m o l–1–(E a R)=–3.27×10 4 K E a=–(8.314 J m o l–1 K–1)(–3.27×10 4 K)=273 k J m o l–1

**Comment**_:_ This activation energy is high, which is not surprising because a carbon-carbon bond must be broken in order to open the cyclopropane ring. (C–C bond energies are typically around 350 kJ/mol.) This is why the reaction must be carried out at high temperature.

### Calculating E a E a without a plot

Because the ln _k_-vs.-1/_T_ plot yields a straight line, it is often convenient to estimate the activation energy from experiments at only two temperatures. To see how this is done, consider that

ln k 2−ln k 1=(ln A−E a R T 2)−(ln A−E a R T 1)=E a R(1 T 1−1 T 2)ln⁡k 2−ln⁡k 1=(ln⁡A−E a R T 2)−(ln⁡A−E a R T 1)=E a R(1 T 1−1 T 2)

The ln-_A_ term is eliminated by subtracting the expressions for the two ln-_k_ terms.) Solving the expression on the right for the activation energy yields

E a=R ln k 2 k 1 1 T 1−1 T 2 E a=R ln⁡k 2 k 1 1 T 1−1 T 2

##### Example 6.2.3.1.2 6.2.3.1.2

A widely used rule-of-thumb for the temperature dependence of a reaction rate is that a **ten degree** rise in the temperature approximately doubles the rate. This is not generally true, especially when a strong covalent bond must be broken. For a reaction that does show this behavior, what would the activation energy be?

enriched_points.expanded_content: The Arrhenius equation models the rate constant's exponential dependence on temperature, illustrated as k = A e^(-Ea/RT), predicting reaction rates effectively across varying conditions.
Determining reaction order involves analyzing how changes in reactant concentration affect the rate, enabling predictions of reaction kinetics through rate laws derived from experimental data.
Temperature significantly impacts reaction rates; a 10°C increase can double the rate, as higher temperatures enhance molecular energy and collision frequency, facilitating reactions.
